dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for Back Pain in [CITY].
Hi, my name is Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Back pain, sharp and shooting down my right leg for 3 days.
What is your name?
What city are we currently in?
What day is it today?
Can you point to this badge with your left hand?
You're alert and oriented x4. That means you know your name, this city, the date, and the reason for calling 911.
Let me ask about the pain. Does it ever go away completely?
Your airway is not obstructed. You are breathing spaciously with no signs of difficulty.
We're taking your blood pressure now. It's currently at 194 over 115.
Your pattern of breathing is normal for now, with no signs of distress.
Your respiratory rate is 14 breaths per minute - regular and even.
Oxygen saturation is at 92%. We'll keep tracking this for changes.
Your pulse is 99 - a bit high but regular. We'll recheck to track it.
My partner is placing a 4-lead EKG now. It should just feel like pressure.
4-lead monitoring is in place. I'm going to run a 12-lead EKG for a closer look.
Tell me, did this back pain begin suddenly or gradually over time?
Have you felt this type of pain before, particularly in your right leg?
When did the pain first start? Try to remember to the best of your ability.
Does moving around make the pain worse?
Yes, moving definitely makes it worse. I can't walk like I normally would.
How would you describe the pain? Try to be as detailed as possible.
The pain goes down your right leg - this is an important finding for our evaluation.
If the worst possible pain is 10, how would you rate your current pain?
Are you allergic to any medications, foods, or things in the environment?
What medications are you currently taking?
Do you have a history of high blood pressure?
Any history of heart or circulatory issues?
What did you eat or drink most recently before the pain started?
Has the pain significantly increased over the past 3 days?
I am taking a blood glucose measurement now - it will only take a moment.
I don't see any rashes, scars, or abrasions along your back and right leg.
Any pain, tenderness, or discomfort in your head or neck?
We found your head and neck to be normal with no signs of injury.
Any drainage or unusual sensations from your ears or nose?
I'm now going to check your chest and lungs. Let me know if it causes any discomfort.
Lung sounds are clear and equal on both sides.
Let me gently press on your abdomen in different areas. Any pain or tenderness?
The abdomen is soft and without signs of pain in all areas.
Any pain or trouble when trying to move your right leg?
12-lead EKG is in progress to get a more detailed view of your heart rhythm.
Given your symptoms, EKG, and medical history, I'll use the pain control protocol. We'll monitor you closely during transport.
Respiratory rate is now 13 breaths - still regular and steady.
Oxygen is up to 98%. A positive change since we started monitoring.
Your pulse is stable at 96 now. Just slightly elevated.
Blood pressure is currently 196 over 118. We're continuing to monitor this carefully.
Earlier blood sugar was 189. We're not repeating it now, but it was stable.
I'm attempting to place an IV in one of your arm or leg veins. You might feel a slight pinch.
Your pulse is at 97 now - still elevated but slightly stabilized.
Blood pressure now is 162 over 117. It's still high but appears to be coming down.
Oxygen saturation is at 97%. Remaining stable.
Has the nature of your pain changed at all since we first spoke?
Would you still rate your pain at 10/10, or is it a little better?
Given your high pain and trouble with your leg, we are heading to [COMPANY_NAME].
I agree with going to the hospital for further care.
We'll position you on the stretcher in a way that causes you the least pain.
You are now fully secured with all harness and restraints in place. You're set for transport.

Patient is 44-year-old male, 141kg. Chief complaint: back pain with radiation to right leg. Responsive (AOx4), GCS 15. No drug allergies, taking Zoloft. History: primary hypertension and pollen allergy. IV access attempt unsuccessful. Transported to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation.
Most recent glucose reading was listed as 0 but was not further evaluated as needed.
IV access in an extremity vein was attempted but unsuccessful after one try.
Respiratory rate was measured at 13 at some earlier point and remains stable.
A later measurement showed a rate of 15 breaths per minute - still normal.
